LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES' MOTION TO QUASH SERVICE OF SUMMONS OF COMPLAINT FOR LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION IS DENIED. PLAINTIFF'S REQUEST FOR JUDICIAL NOTICE IS NOT OPPOSED AND IS GRANTED PURSUANT TO EVID. CODE 452 (C), (D) AND (H). THE CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION MADE FINDINGS THAT MR. HINES WAIVED ANY DEFENSE OF A LACK OF PERSONAL JURISDICTION BY APPEARING AND TESTIFYING. FURTHER IT IS UNDISPUTED THAT HE DID SO TESTIFY. (EXHIBIT B, RJN.) THE CPUC MADE A FURTHER FINDING THAT MR. HINES WAS THE ALTER-EGO OF TELESEVEN, LLC AND CALLING 10 LLC DBA CALIFORNIA CALLING 10. THE CPUC AND THE FCC FOUND THAT MR. HINES WAS ULTIMATELY RESPONSIBLE FOR PUTTING GOODS AND SERVICES INTO THE STREAM OF COMMERCE, SUCH THAT HE KNEW, OR SHOULD HAVE KNOWN, THAT THEY ENDED IN CALIFORNIA AND ON CALIFORNIA PHONE BILLS. (SEE EXHIBIT C TO REQUEST FOR JUDICIAL NOTICE.) THESE SUBSTANTIVE FINDINGS CANNOT BE REVIEWED BY THIS COURT. (PUB. UTIL. CODE 1759.) THESE FINDINGS WERE NOT REVERSED BY THE COURT OF APPEAL OR THE SUPREME COURT AND THEREFORE THIS COURT IS BOUND BY THE CPUC'S DETERMINATION. EVEN IF IT WAS NOT SO BOUND, BASED ON THE EVIDENTIARY RECORD BEFORE IT ACCOMPANYING THE MOTION TO QUASH, THIS COURT FINDS THAT PLAINTIFF HAS ESTABLISHED THAT MINIMUM CONTACTS AND ALL JURISDICTIONAL CRITERIA AGAINST MR. HINES ARE MET. WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED AND FILED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: ANGELA BRADSTREET; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: MARIA VISCIO, CSR NO. 12353, EMAIL: MVISCIO@SFTC.ORG. (D302)

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES' DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED IN ITS ENTIRETY. MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Christofer Nolan, Esq. of the CPUC Legal Division, T:415.696.7303, for plaintiff/moving party;
Joel Dichter, Esq. of Joe Dichter Law Offices, T:212.593.4202, for defendant/opposing party Patrick Hines, on court call.

PLAINTIFF THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling. The People of the State of California's motion for judgment on the pleadings as to its complaint and all fourteen affirmative defenses in the answer filed by defendant Patrick Hines is granted without leave to amend and its request for prejudgment interest is denied. (See word minutes for details of the court's ruling.)

ORDER and JUDGMENT SIGNED in open court.

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons Of Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
Off Calendar Aug-03-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Christophe P. Witteman of the Public Utilities Commission of the State of California (415-355-5524) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Joel Dichter of the law offices of Dichter Law LLC (212-593-4202) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES' Motion To Quash Service Of Summons Of Complaint For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction is DENIED. Plaintiff's Request for Judicial Notice is not opposed and is granted pursuant to Evid. Code 452 (c), (d) and (h). The California Public Utilities Commission made findings that Mr. Hines waived any defense of a lack of personal jurisdiction by appearing and testifying. Further it is undisputed that he did so testify. (Exhibit B, RJN.) The CPUC made a further finding that Mr. Hines was the alter-ego of Teleseven, LLC and Calling 10 LLC dba California Calling 10. The CPUC and the FCC found that Mr. Hines was ultimately responsible for putting goods and services into the stream of commerce, such that he knew, or should have known, that they ended in California and on California phone bills. (See Exhibit C to Request for Judicial Notice.) These substantive findings cannot be reviewed by this court. (Pub. Util. Code 1759.) These findings were not reversed by the Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court and therefore this court is bound by the CPUC's determination. Even if it was not so bound, based on the evidentiary record before it accompanying the motion to quash, this Court finds that Plaintiff has established that minimum contacts and all jurisdictional criteria against Mr. Hines are met. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Angela Bradstreet; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Viscio, CSR No. 12353, email: mviscio@sftc.org. (D302)

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-03-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Christophe P. Witteman of the Public Utilities Commission of the State of California (415-355-5524) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Joel Dichter of the law offices of Dichter Law LLC (212-593-4202) appears as counsel for defendant/demurring party

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT DEFENDANT PATRICK HINES' DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is overruled in its entirety. The Court does not need to determine here whether in fact CCP 340(b) applies to this action or whether there is no statute of limitations that limits CPUC's ability under section 2104 to collect on fines it assesses, other than being limited by the equitable doctrine of laches, for purposes of this demurrer. The Court merely notes here that the subject August 14 decision attached to the Complaint specifically provided that fines were being assessed against Mr. Hines "pursuant to Sections 2107 and 2108." (Id. at p. 42, conclusion of law #7.) 2108 of the California Public Utilities Code provides, in pertinent part, that "every violation of the provisions of this part or of any part of any order, decision'by'any person is a separate and distinct offense, and in case of a continuing violation each day's continuance thereof shall be a separate and distinct offense." Thus, this provision therefore may control pursuant to CCP 312. Further, the Court is not convinced that CCP 340(b) even generally applies to actions to recover fines or penalties that have previously been awarded but not yet collected, as opposed to actions seeking penalties in the first place. However, the Court does not need to request additional briefing since it does not need to decide these issues for purposes of this demurrer. Assuming, arguendo, that CCP 340(b) does apply, as argued by moving party, the court finds that the Plaintiff has alleged sufficient facts to assert that such statute of limitations was equitably tolled and / or that Defendant is equitably estopped from asserting such defense. The complaint at para. 19 alleges that review was denied by the California appellate and supreme courts by November 18, 2015. The Complaint further alleges that Plaintiff's prior demand for payment after the decision had been met with an objection by the Defendant that such demand was not yet ripe. (Complaint para. 20.) Within a year of the final appellate determination, Plaintiff filed this action. As to Mr. Hines' arguments of preemption and the imposition of an excessive fine, those arguments require this court to "review, reverse, correct or annul" the decision made by the CPUC, which it is barred from doing pursuant to Pub. Util. Code 1759. Moreover, Mr. Hines made the same arguments before the Court of Appeal and our Supreme Court and their refusal to overturn the CPUC's decision means this Court must also accept the CPUC's findings. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Angela Bradstreet; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Viscio, CSR No. 12353, email: mviscio@sftc.org. (D302)
